name: Breathing Permit of Hôr
shortDescription: A late Egyptian funerary text ensuring continued existence and breath in the afterlife for the deceased.
description: The Breathing Permit of Hôr is a late Egyptian funerary text, dating primarily from the Ptolemaic and Roman periods (c. 300 BCE - 300 CE). It is a shortened and altered version of earlier funerary texts, notably the Book of the Dead. Unlike the Book of the Dead, which contained a wide range of spells and rituals, the Breathing Permit focused primarily on ensuring the deceased continued to breathe and exist in the afterlife. The text promised the deceased continued sustenance and freedom of movement within the realm of the dead, protecting them from annihilation and ensuring their eternal existence. The Breathing Permit was often inscribed on papyrus and placed within the coffin or near the mummy of the deceased, serving as a magical guarantee of their continued vitality in the next world. It reflects evolving funerary beliefs and the increasing commodification of afterlife assurances during the later periods of Egyptian history.
subtitle: Eternal Breath for the Deceased
author: Various Priests and Scribes
period: Ptolemaic and Roman Periods (c. 300 BCE - 300 CE)
significance: Demonstrates evolving Egyptian beliefs about death and the afterlife during the Ptolemaic and Roman periods.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Mythology
extendedContent.content: The Breathing Permit of Hôr emerged from a long tradition of Egyptian funerary texts, evolving from the Pyramid Texts of the Old Kingdom to the Coffin Texts of the Middle Kingdom and the Book of the Dead of the New Kingdom. As Egyptian civilization interacted more intensely with other cultures during the Ptolemaic and Roman periods, funerary beliefs shifted and simplified. The Breathing Permit represents this shift, offering a concise and accessible means of securing afterlife benefits. The mythology surrounding the Breathing Permit centers on the idea that breath is essential for continued existence, even in the afterlife. By invoking specific deities and reciting particular phrases, the text aimed to magically guarantee that the deceased would continue to breathe, eat, and move freely in the realm of the dead, avoiding the perils of non-existence.

extendedContent.title: Role & Significance
extendedContent.content: The Breathing Permit played a crucial role in Egyptian funerary practices during the Ptolemaic and Roman periods. It was believed to provide the deceased with the necessary tools and knowledge to navigate the afterlife successfully. By ensuring continued breath and vitality, the text aimed to protect the deceased from the dangers of the underworld, such as demons, monsters, and the threat of annihilation. The Breathing Permit also served as a status symbol, demonstrating the wealth and piety of the deceased and their family. The inclusion of the text in the burial demonstrated a commitment to traditional Egyptian beliefs and a desire to secure the best possible afterlife for the deceased. The text's concise nature and relatively low cost compared to complete copies of the Book of the Dead made it accessible to a wider range of the population.

extendedContent.title: Symbolism & Interpretation
extendedContent.content: The Breathing Permit is rich in symbolism, drawing upon traditional Egyptian beliefs about the afterlife and the power of words. Breath, in particular, is a central symbol, representing life, vitality, and the ability to interact with the world. The text's emphasis on continued breathing signifies the deceased's ongoing existence and their ability to participate in the activities of the afterlife. The deities invoked in the text, such as Osiris, Isis, and Nephthys, represent protection, resurrection, and the cyclical nature of life and death. The specific phrases and spells included in the Breathing Permit were believed to possess magical power, capable of influencing the fate of the deceased in the afterlife. The text can be interpreted as a testament to the Egyptian belief in the power of ritual, language, and divine intervention to overcome death and achieve eternal life.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: While the Breathing Permit of Hôr is not as widely known as the Book of the Dead, it offers valuable insights into the evolution of Egyptian funerary beliefs during the later periods of Egyptian history. The text's emphasis on breath as a symbol of life and continued existence resonates with broader themes of resurrection and immortality found in many cultures. The Breathing Permit has been studied by Egyptologists for its unique content and its reflection of the changing social and religious landscape of ancient Egypt. The text also serves as a reminder of the enduring human desire to overcome death and achieve eternal life. Its influence, though subtle, can be seen in modern interpretations of ancient Egyptian religion and in contemporary discussions about death, dying, and the afterlife. The text's focus on personal salvation and individual access to the afterlife anticipates later religious developments in the Mediterranean world.

sources.author: Mark Smith
sources.work: Traversing the Netherworld: The Book of Two Ways
sources.description: Analysis of Egyptian funerary literature and its evolution.

sources.author: Emily Teeter
sources.work: Religion and Ritual in Ancient Egypt
sources.description: Overview of Egyptian religious beliefs and practices, including funerary rituals.

associations.name: Breath
associations.significance: Represents life, vitality, and continued existence in the afterlife.

associations.name: Papyrus
associations.significance: The material on which the Breathing Permit was typically written.

associations.name: Osiris
associations.significance: God of the afterlife, resurrection, and regeneration.

cross_cultural_parallels.name: Tibetan Book of the Dead
cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: A funerary text guiding the deceased through the afterlife.

cross_cultural_parallels.name: Christian Requiem Mass
cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Prayers and rituals performed to ensure the soul of the deceased finds peace in the afterlife.

companions.name: Osiris
companions.description: God of the afterlife, often invoked in the Breathing Permit for protection.

companions.name: Anubis
companions.description: God of embalming and the dead, associated with funerary rituals.

worshipPractices.name: Funerary Rituals
worshipPractices.description: Rituals performed to prepare the deceased for the afterlife, including embalming, offering, and reciting spells.

worshipPractices.name: Offering to Deities
worshipPractices.description: Offerings of food, drink, and other goods made to the gods to ensure their favor and protection of the deceased.

festivals.name: Wepet Renpet
festivals.description: Celebration of the new year, associated with regeneration and renewal, relevant to afterlife beliefs.

cultural.modernLegacy: The Breathing Permit contributes to our understanding of ancient Egyptian views on death, the afterlife, and the human desire for immortality. It continues to be studied by Egyptologists and inspires contemporary art and literature.
